does: This paper introduces Discrete Denoising Posterior Prediction (DDPP), a framework for controlling the generation of discrete data from masked diffusion models without requiring reinforcement learning or differentiable rewards. The method reformulates steering pre-trained MDMs as Bayesian posterior inference, learning to sample from a posterior distribution that satisfies specified properties or reward functions. The authors derive three novel training objectives that are simulation-free and scalable, capable of handling arbitrary non-differentiable reward functions. They demonstrate DDPP on multiple tasks including class-conditional image generation and RLHF-based alignment of discrete sequences.
connects: This work builds on masked diffusion models (MDMs), which have recently emerged as alternatives to autoregressive approaches for discrete data generation. It extends prior research on controlling generative models—particularly RLHF methods developed for large language models—to the discrete diffusion setting. The paper connects to broader literature on conditioning and steering diffusion models, probabilistic inference approaches to generation, and posterior sampling techniques. It responds to limitations of existing discrete generative models by offering a scalable alternative to RLHF that avoids simulation costs and handles non-differentiable objectives, bridging classical Bayesian inference with modern diffusion-based generation.
enables: This framework opens new directions for controllable generation across domains where discrete MDMs apply: protein sequence design, text generation for language agents, and other discrete data applications. The simulation-free, reward-agnostic nature of DDPP could enable more efficient alignment of generative models compared to RLHF, potentially making advanced control techniques accessible for expensive-to-evaluate objectives like protein function or molecular properties. The work suggests pathways for combining principled Bayesian posterior inference with modern deep generative models, which could inform future research on alignment, interpretability, and safe generation of discrete sequences in high-stakes applications.
